title: Conservation of Energy
summary: Energy changes form but never disappears — balance it with and without friction.

steps.title: Energy is never lost
steps.body:
Energy doesn't appear or disappear — it just changes form. A roller coaster at the top of the hill is all **potential energy**; at the bottom it's all **kinetic**. The total never changed.

This is the big idea of the whole unit.

steps.title: The rule
steps.body: When only **conservative** forces (like gravity or springs) act, mechanical energy stays constant — KE and PE just trade back and forth.

steps.title: Get a feel for it
steps.body: Slide the ball down the frictionless ramp. Watch potential energy pour into kinetic energy — while the total bar never moves.

steps.prompt: A ball is released from rest at the top of a frictionless ramp. At the bottom, compared to the top, it has…
choices: The same total energy, now all kinetic
More energy than it started with
hints: No friction is the key here — what does that mean for the total mechanical energy?
The energy isn't created or destroyed: the PE_g it gives up reappears as KE. Same total, just a new form.
steps.solution:
On a frictionless ramp, mechanical energy is conserved — none is created or destroyed. The gravitational PE the ball gives up on the way down reappears as kinetic energy, so the total stays the same and is now all kinetic at the bottom.

**So the answer is "The same total energy, now all kinetic".**

steps.title: Work through one
steps.body:
A **2 kg** block starts at rest at **h = 2 m** on a frictionless ramp.

At the top: PE_g = mgh = 2 × 9.8 × 2 = 39.2 J, KE = 0.
At the bottom: all of it is kinetic → KE = **39.2 J**.

Your turn.

steps.prompt: A **2 kg** block starts at rest at **h = 2 m** and slides down a **frictionless** ramp. Drag the **KE** at the bottom.
hints: No friction means the PE_g it loses becomes KE it gains.
Find the PE_g at the top with m × g × h — that whole amount shows up as KE at the bottom.
steps.solution:
With no friction, all the gravitational PE at the top becomes kinetic energy at the bottom: KE = PE_g.

PE_g = mgh = 2 × 9.8 × 2 = 39.2 J, so KE = **39.2 J**.

steps.title: When friction joins in
steps.body: Friction doesn't destroy energy either — it turns mechanical energy into **thermal energy (E_th)**, which heats things up. So now the books balance like this:

steps.prompt: Same drop, but friction made **E_th = 19.6 J**. Starting from **PE_g = 39.2 J**, drag the **KE** left at the bottom.
hints: Friction turns some energy into heat, so the books balance as PE_g = KE + E_th.
Rearrange to KE = PE_g − E_th, then subtract the thermal energy from the starting PE_g.
steps.solution:
With friction, energy balances as PE_g = KE + E_th. Rearranging gives KE = PE_g − E_th.

KE = 39.2 − 19.6 = **19.6 J**.

steps.title: Lesson complete!
steps.body: You balanced energy with and without friction — conserved when it applies, turned to heat when friction acts.
